name: Miso Butter Ramen with Soft-Boiled Egg
author: Henry
why: White miso and brown butter create an incredibly rich, umami-packed broth that's surprisingly quick. The technique of browning butter before adding miso unlocks nutty depth you won't find in standard ramen recipes. Perfect for a Friday night when you want something restaurant-quality without the fuss.
ingredients: 200g fresh ramen noodles or thick egg noodles

steps: Soft-boil eggs: Lower gently into simmering water, time 6 minutes 30 seconds, then transfer to ice water. Peel when cool.
Mince garlic and grate ginger.
Slice spring onions thinly on the diagonal.
Cut nori sheet into 4 thin strips.
Melt butter in a medium saucepan over medium heat. Watch carefully — it will foam, then subside, then turn amber and smell nutty (about 2-3 minutes). Do not walk away.
Remove from heat briefly. Whisk in miso paste until smooth.
Return to low heat. Add garlic and ginger, cook 30 seconds until fragrant.
Pour in stock and water, whisk to dissolve miso fully. Add soy sauce and mirin.
Simmer gently for 5 minutes. Do not boil vigorously — it breaks down the miso.
Bring a large pot of salted water to the boil. Cook ramen noodles according to package (usually 2-3 minutes for fresh).
Drain noodles well. Divide between 2 warm bowls.
Ladle hot broth over noodles.
Halve soft-boiled eggs and place on top.
Garnish with spring onions, nori strips, and sesame seeds.
Add a drizzle of chili oil if you want heat.

pro_tips: The key is browning the butter slowly — the nutty compounds (methylpyrazines) it develops pair perfectly with miso's glutamates for maximum umami impact.
If you can't find fresh ramen, thick spaghetti works fine. Just reduce cook time by 1 minute and reserve some pasta water to thin the broth.

name: Grilled Peach and Burrata Salad
author: Mart
ingredients: 1 tbsp rice vinegar or white wine vinegar
8 oz burrata, brought to room temperature
2 oz thinly sliced prosciutto, torn into pieces
Freshly ground black pepper, to taste

steps: Select peaches that are ripe but still firm — they should yield slightly to gentle pressure but not feel mushy. Overripe peaches will fall apart on the grill; underripe ones won't soften or sweeten properly.
Pit and quarter the peaches (cut into quarters, not halves — this exposes more surface for char and makes flipping easier).
Bring the burrata to room temperature at least 30 minutes before serving. Cold burrata won't spread and mutes its richness.
Whisk together the olive oil, rice vinegar, fish sauce, and minced chives in a small bowl. Set aside.
Set up a two-zone fire on your grill.
Charcoal: Fill a chimney starter ¾ full. Once ashed over, pour coals evenly over half the grate. Leave the other half bare.
Gas: Preheat all burners on high (~500°F) for 15 minutes. Keep one burner on high, turn off the other(s) to create an indirect zone.
Clean and oil the grate thoroughly.
Brush the cut sides of the peach quarters with melted butter.
Place peaches cut-side down on the hot zone. Cook 5–7 minutes until deep grill marks form.

Flip the peaches and move them to the cooler zone. Cook another 3–7 minutes until tender, lightly marked on the second side, and slightly translucent at the edges. Total grill time is about 10 minutes.
Transfer peaches to a plate and let them cool for a few minutes.
Place the burrata on a large platter. Cut or tear it into 8–10 pieces so the creamy stracciatella center can ooze across the plate.
Arrange the warm peach quarters around and over the burrata.
Scatter the torn prosciutto and fresh basil across the top.
Drizzle the vinaigrette evenly over everything.
Finish with a generous crack of black pepper.
Serve immediately — the contrast of warm peaches, room-temp burrata, and crisp herbs is best right at assembly.

why: Grilled peaches bring caramelised sweetness to a salad that balances rich burrata, salty prosciutto and bright herbs.
